sound_type: deep owl-like coo-coo -- surprisingly low-pitched, resonant cooing at 300-600 Hz from high in the forest canopy; sounds more like an owl than a pigeon; intermittent; E=3
ecosystem: Coniferous and mixed forests of the PNW. Nests high in the canopy. Feeds on fruits, nuts, and grain. Congregates at mineral springs for geophagy. The native wild pigeon of western North America. Larger and more colorful than the Rock Pigeon.
season: Summer breeder in PNW. Calling peaks May-August. Some year-round residents in lowland areas. Winters in California and Mexico for mountain populations.
family: Columbidae (1st entry. FIRST COLUMBIDAE. Opens the four-degree Columbidae quartet 149-152.)

coordinates.description: Band-tailed Pigeons inhabit coniferous and mixed forests throughout the Cascades and Olympics. Mineral spring sites in western Washington are traditional gathering points.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST Columbidae entry. Opens four-degree Columbidae quartet (149-152): Band-tailed Pigeon (native forest), Mourning Dove (native open), Eurasian Collared-Dove (invasive), Rock Pigeon wing clap (feral mechanical). Native-to-non-native progression. Largest pigeon in North America.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Patagioenas from Greek 'patagion' (gold border on robe) + Latin 'columba' (dove) -- referring to the iridescent nape. fasciata from Latin 'fascia' (band) -- referring to the gray tail band.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Patagioenas -- first entry. The 'New World pigeons' -- large, forest-dwelling.
taxonomy_note.family_position: First Columbidae. Opens the quartet: native forest (149), native open (150), invasive (151), feral mechanical (152).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Coo: 300-600 Hz. Surprisingly deep for a pigeon. Owl-like quality.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Cooing: 300-600 Hz. Low-frequency. Carries through forest but feels muted.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual coo: 1.0-1.5 seconds. Repeated 3-6 times with 2-3 second pauses. Then silence.
song_metrics.note_structure: DEEP COO-COO: Two notes, second slightly higher and more emphatic. 'coo-COO' or 'whoo-OOO'. Rich, resonant, breathy.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Two-note phrase repeated 3-6 times with pauses. Intermittent. Sporadic calling creates ambient forest texture.
song_metrics.timbre: Rich, resonant, breathy. Owl-like depth. Produced by syrinx and amplified by inflated crop.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Moderate at source but perceived as quiet from ground level (canopy height 20-40m). Audible 100-200m in forest. Diffuse, atmospheric.
song_metrics.tempo: Slow. 3-6 repetitions per calling bout. Long pauses. Intermittent throughout the day during breeding season.
song_metrics.context: Males call from high perches during breeding season (May-August). Territorial advertisement and mate attraction. Outside breeding season, infrequent.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Deep cooing consistent across Pacific coast range.
song_metrics.key_property: DEEP OWL-LIKE COO-COO -- 300-600 Hz, two notes from forest canopy, intermittent, often mistaken for an owl. E=3. FIRST COLUMBIDAE. Native forest pigeon. Mineral spring congregations. Declining populations. Passenger Pigeon parallel. Seed dispersal. Largest NA pigeon.

plumage.overall: Purple-tinged breast. Blue-gray back. Yellow bill with dark tip. White crescent on back of neck (diagnostic). Gray tail band. Iridescent green-purple nape. Far more colorful than Rock Pigeon.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: White crescent on back of neck. Yellow bill. Large size. Forest habitat.
plumage.flight_pattern: Strong, direct flight. Wing clap on takeoff. Fast and powerful in open air.

physical_description.overall: Length: 33-40 cm. Weight: 300-450 g. Largest pigeon in North America. Significantly larger than Rock Pigeon (240-380g average).
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: White neck crescent. Yellow bill. Large size. Forest canopy habitat separates from all other PNW pigeons/doves.
physical_description.field_id: Large pigeon in forest canopy with purple breast, white neck crescent, and yellow bill. Deep cooing from high perches. At mineral springs: flocks of dozens to hundreds.

nodes.title: FIRST COLUMBIDAE -- Family Opens
nodes.description: The pigeon/dove family enters the engine. Four-degree Columbidae quartet (149-152): native forest, native open, invasive, feral mechanical. A progression from native to non-native, from vocalization to mechanical sound.

nodes.title: The Native Pigeon -- Not Your City Pigeon
nodes.description: The native wild pigeon of western North America. Larger, more colorful, more ecologically significant than the Rock Pigeon. Forest canopy. Declining. Unknown to most people who see 'pigeons' every day.

nodes.title: Mineral Spring Congregations
nodes.description: Flocks gather at traditional mineral spring sites for geophagy -- drinking mineral water and consuming mineral-rich soil. Sodium supplement for fruit/nut diet. Same sites used for generations. Community behavior.

nodes.title: Declining Populations -- Passenger Pigeon Parallel
nodes.description: Historical abundance destroyed by market hunting (especially at mineral springs) and habitat loss. Population declining 2-3% per year. Passenger Pigeon parallel: same pattern of overhunting concentrated flocks. 1.5-2M remaining.

cross_references.relationship: NATIVE CONTRAST PAIR! Band-tailed (forest canopy, declining, 300-450g) vs. Mourning Dove (open habitat, thriving, 100-170g). Both native. Both E=3. Different ecology.
NATIVE vs. INVASIVE! Band-tailed in forest, Collared-Dove in urban. Minimal direct competition. Different habitats protect the native.
NATIVE vs. FERAL! The original pigeon vs. the ubiquitous replacement. Band-tailed (native, forest, declining) vs. Rock Pigeon (feral, urban, 260M global). The displacement story.
FAMILY TRANSITION! Apodidae (swifts, aerial) to Columbidae (pigeons, terrestrial/arboreal). From aerial insectivory to ground-based herbivory.
